sentences.text: We were starting to pack our cars, all the major belongings in there, locked the house, and left, and left towards France.
The whole family-- my auntie, my uncle, and the baby and me, and the mother-- the auntie's mother.
We were not the only ones.
Thousands of cars left, tried to run away, because the Germans approaching towards-- towards Brussels already.
No, everybody.
Everybody who had a bit of wheels and whatever were trying to leave the-- the country.
The troops were collapsing already on the-- on the-- on the-- on the [INAUDIBLE] line.
Well, we went towards La Panne, place called La Panne.
Well, we saw troop movement from the Belgians trying to go towards the front.
And we were-- we were machine gunned by the German aeroplanes on the road.
The columns of refugees were machine gunned by stukas, which came down with a tremendous noise onto the road to machine gun people.
Some people were dying in their cars, and just like that, you know?
And some people shot through and-- and-- and the speed, and some people run in the woods.
When their machine gun near La Panne, I took the baby and I run and zigzag.
They machine gunned the road.
I didn't know where my uncle was.
I didn't know where my auntie was.
I just took the baby and run.
And I finished up in a windmill with the baby.
Then after, I met with my uncle.
I found my uncle.
Well, in the woods.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then-- and then we went further into France.
We found her too.
She was-- everybody was all right in our car.
We went into France.
And then we couldn't go anymore.
They stopped us.
The war was finished in Belgium.
And the German occupied Belgium.
No.
And we had to-- we turned back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't.
I stayed.
I went into France.
My uncle turned back, my auntie turned back, and the baby turned back, and the mother.
I kept on going.
I kept on going into south France.
Over-- into Paris.
No.
No.
They-- they wanted to go back home to their place.
Yeah.
More or less.
And I kept on going by myself.
And I reach Paris, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which wasn't occupied then, but nearly, you know?
And from Paris, I went right through into south of France.
And I-- on the-- on the right hand side, near Hossegor, near Bordeaux.
Not much.
I-- I lifted, lifts, with troops, with this, with that, and with private people and things like that.
And I reached-- I reached Hossegor.
You see, I was telling them that I-- there was in Lyon-- they were enlisting Belgians who flee to France, and were enlisting them for the army to send them back to fighting against the Germans.
So I said, I-- I will do that.
So they helped me.
And I reached a place called Hossegor, which is near Saint-Jean-de-Luz on the Pyrenees.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's where I stayed.
And all of a sudden, a week later or two weeks later-- it was a holiday of sort-- Germans were there.
Not really the date.
The 18th-- the 18th of June was all finished. 10th of May, 18th of June-- so the whole thing lasted 30-odd days.
And the Germans were there.
So I wanted to go into Spain-- run into Spain.
I couldn't get through.
The Falange was there.
They are fascists, you know?
Franco was there in that time.
And then I want to reach the Republic Andova, which is a separate republic in the Pyrenes.
Anyhow, there were too many-- too many of those.
They were shooting at people and things like that.
So I went back to the fisher village.
And the Germans were there.
And I stole a German motorbike.
Didn't know what I was doing-- filled it up with petrol and-- and-- and run back to Paris on a German motorbike.
Did you have any papers, any documents on you?
No.
None.
The only thing, I spoke German fluently.
So I bluffed them with it, because it was the Wehrmacht.
The Wehrmacht are the regular army, so them, I could bluff.
They were not Gestapo, [INAUDIBLE].
I was young kid, you know.
Then I-- I abandoned the motorbikes right away, because I saw that Paris is controlled by police and everything else.
And I found my way back to Belgium [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and came back home to my uncle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I was working as a portier in a nightclub, in a German nightclub.
It was not a [INAUDIBLE] nightclub.
It was a nightclub.
There were girls downstairs, you know-- bar girls, pick up girls.
And I was having a uniform, a red uniform of a portier, and I was standing outside.
And I was screaming in three languages for the Germans to come in-- in German, in French, and in Flemish-- to bring them into the club.
Now-- and I got money for that.
The only trouble is to go home, because you see, you-- you could not walk after 10 o'clock in the night time.
All.
Curfew-- and I came home at 3 o'clock in the morning.
And I had to hide in-- in niches, because the Feldgendarmerie was everywhere, you know, death, where they picked you up.
And my uncle thought I'm going to jeopardize him.
And I had a tremendous row with him, you know.
He was even physical towards me in some stages.
But I needed pocket money.
I wanted money to get out.
I was determined to get out.
Huh?
Where?
To Spain.
And then-- and from Spain to England.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were often what you call [INAUDIBLE].
They were encircling the streets and then they were picking up people, but not at night club, because the Germans were there.
And one day, they gave me passes to give to taxi driver.
I didn't know what was in the passes.
I just gave him the passes, and I had connection with the taxi driver.
What they were actually doing is they were, while the Germans were with the girls, they had to leave their pistols and their uniforms in the vestibule.
And the girls from the clock room removed the guns and the caps, and the Germans were drunk when they come out anyhow, and that went to the underground.
[LAUGHS] I didn't know.
And one day, they were raiding the whole nightclub.
And-- and I had to run away, naturally, never came back.
They were arresting everybody there.
I just ran, ran through the street-- I knew my streets, and I knew my little side streets.
And--
No.
No.
I-- I-- I could hear the-- you see, the Germans were stupid.
They were always walking in-- in eins, zwei, drei, in a-- in a step, you know?
And you could hear them for miles in the-- in the cobblestones.
You could--
Oh, yeah.
The Feldgendarmerie with the placards, you know?
And taxis at that particular time, they're going on fire, you know, fire, gas, fire, gas, certain gas, combustion gas.
And then I said to myself, it's time to get out.
I think they were even been looking for me as a portier [INAUDIBLE].
And my auntie gave me some money-- a few hundred dollars or whatever it was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I dyed my hair blonde with peroxide, my eyebrows blonde.
I got hold of a uniform.
Before that, I was also working in the NSKK.
That means Nationalsozialistisches Kraftfahrkorps.
That means cleaning cars and like chauffeur, you know?
They were hiring civil people.
And they-- special armbands.
And there, when I was working there, as a, you know, sort of second great mechanic, I was stealing uniforms for myself.
And I put them on.
And I went on the army train, only-- only for army, you know?
As a German soldier.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
And I stole papers too.
I went on the train to Paris.
From their-- from their-- from their glove boxes and things like that, their uniforms, their glove boxes, overalls.
And I made myself a letter on a letterhead from the NSKK, which I stole in the office, that I am in charge of-- of picking up-- delivering-- picking up spare parts for trucks.
And I had that in my pocket, signed, everything, stamped.
I got-- it was all right.
And then I went in a German train.
And I bought [INAUDIBLE], which is a [? nationalistic ?] paper.
And I sat there and I read it.
And opposite me, there was a high officer with a bandage around from Stalingrad or somewhere, I don't know where from.
And the-- the compartment was full with soldiers and officers and-- going-- and leave from the front.
And the train went to Paris.
And I passed Paris.
There was no civilian on that train.
I passed Paris.
And maybe I should have got out in Paris.

Anyway, I passed Paris, and all of a sudden, before the train comes in the [INAUDIBLE] line to Vichy, to unoccupied-- unoccupied France-- that was Vichy, France, who was sympathetic to [INAUDIBLE] to General [INAUDIBLE] and all that.
They were picking up Jews in there too, by the way.
And the Feldgendarmerie was coming on the train to control the papers, if anybody is deserter or-- or unlawful on the train without any-- or run away from the front and sits there.
Now, when it came to me, I showed the papers, and the passport.
Was-- everything was all right, except one thing.
I made a mistake.
I was too young.
A cigarette piece of paper-- I smoked cigarettes.
I rolled it.
And on that cigarette piece of paper, I had a lot of addresses-- from Israel, from New York, and from London.
And I put it in my-- in one of the passports.
And as I showed him, it fell down.
And I went to pick it up, and he put-- [BACKROUND NOISE] put the boot on it.
And he picked it up.
And then I knew.
And about five minutes-- seven minutes later, the train stopped.
It was encircled by-- by an army.
They came on the plane with [? stolen ?] pistols.
I didn't know where to run-- the toilet?
They grabbed me.
And then they put me in a car.
Did they know who you were at that stage?
No.
To Gestapo headquarters.
At Lille.
No, in Paris-- in Paris it was-- in Paris Gestapo headquarter.
And there was an officer with me.
And there was a driver.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the officer had a dossier for me, somewhere-- I had to-- the questions he asked me.
In that time, I got rid of the-- of those papers.
I threw them out, out of-- out of the train already before.
But I also had a Belgium identity card with me.
But I got rid of the German papers.
And I showed him the Belgium card.
And I was sitting in the back of the car.
It was a DKW, you know?
Audi-- an Audi, but the old one, with the-- with the gears changing like that.
And I was in the back with cuffs.
And as the man walked to the Gestapo headquarters, and the driver went for a pee on the tree, I jumped from the backseat to the front seat, started the car to get off.
The Gestapo car-- and he took the pistol and shot in the tires, and then he hit me behind the ear here.
See all this?
I don't know.
You can't see that.
You can see it.
And I was bleeding.
And they took me to get a bandage.
And then they put me in the prison, in jail in Lille.
And then they put on my cell that I'm dangerous, a dangerous prisoner.
And I had the lights on the whole night in the cell.
And I to took-- take my pants off and my shoes and put it outside.
No.
They told me I'm gonna get shot any minute as a spy.
I'm a spy.
And all they wanted to know-- they beat me up.
And they put the records on.
And they start beating and [INAUDIBLE] in-- in me.
And they want to know what the addresses were from London, from New York, and from Palestine.
And I told them I'm a Jew.
And I had to take my pants off and show it to them.
They wouldn't believe it.
And they still didn't believe it.
And I said, I'm a Jew.
I wanted to escape.
I said I want to study.
The only reasons I couldn't study in Germany is I wanted to study in Switzerland.
They bought the story in the end.
Otherwise, they would-- otherwise, I would have been shot as a spy without any mercy, if I wouldn't be Jewish.
But that was a good excuse for me that explained it all.
And well, after a bit of torturing and a few teeth knocked out and things like that, they--
Mm.
Mm.
And they put me in a cell there, cement cell, and all sorts of thing.
And then they transported me to-- back to Brussels in a van, in a prisoner van.
And they put me in a jail called Saint Gilles.
That was a normal jail, like here, [?
Pentridge. ?] But it was taken over by the Wehrmacht.
Oh, they put the-- they were all people on the resistance.
They're not just Jews.
It was a jail.
And priests-- I had one priest in my cell, a Catholic, monsignor.
Yeah.
That was end of '41.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he got shot.
They took him out of the cell, never came back again.
They shot him.
He was-- he was letting pigeons go to London from his church.
[LAUGHS] And he wanted to, you know-- he knew I was Jewish, but he was trying very hard to convince me.
I said, look, first, before you try to very hard to convince me, I said, you-- you look after yourself and you wash yourself a bit more, and I won't look when you wash.
And he didn't want to undress.
He was very shy, but a very nice guy.
And then I had another fellow who laughed all the time.
He went completely mad.
He blew up a train.
He got shot too, naturally.
And he explained me how the Germans all flew in the-- in the air while he was blowing up the train with dynamite.
You know, people like that.
I was allowed 10 minutes' walk in the courtyard with my hands in the back on my-- my head, 10 minutes.
And then they locked me back in.
I was taken off into interrogations.
The Gestapo came and get certain numbers out interrogations.
And that's the way I started to.
One fellow was always beating and the other one offered me cigarettes, came in, and offered me cigarettes.
Particularly, I remember his name was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his name was Oberscharfuhrer [?
Rutenbaum-- ?] that was from the Gestapo.
He [INAUDIBLE]-- how long I was there?
Eight months.
That was eight months.
And then from there on, after a while, they released me into the deportation camp.
All the Jews were taken out of the jail.
Malines.
They emptied the jail from Jews.
All the others stayed.
Oh, there were shootings going-- executions going on every morning.
We heard it in the courtyard.
Yeah.
That was a transit camp.
There were women, children.
Everything was there.
Very good.
Fantastic.
Oh, too nice.
Oh, the-- the-- the-- the SS were flirting with the Jewish women.
The Jewish women thought because they're flirting they won't go.
Wherever they had to go-- to Germany to work.
Some of them slept with them because they thought being in favor with them, they would let them go, which is--
Oh, well, we know that.
Oh, no-- I-- I wasn't the type of a peeping Tom.
But we knew they were on.
They get special foods and they had more than anybody else.
Food was plenty of food, because-- plenty of food because the Jews were bringing food.
If you know our people, wherever they go, they're-- just in case, they bring a few tins and the food with them.
So the-- the-- the-- what you call it, the kitchen was full with food from people who came from [? home ?] not from jails, and brought food over there.
About a week, 10 days.
No.
Sometimes loading a truck or something like that with food, but they wanted a few volunteers.
I'm talking about February 1942.
And transports were going every week to work to Germany.
We supposed to work in factories and industry, et cetera, et cetera.
That's all we knew.
And there, you know, together with the wives and the children, and they give you accommodation.
And the wife is working in a factory.
And the-- and that's what all we knew.
We didn't know anything else-- somewhere in Germany, somewhere maybe in Poland.
Oh, they were worried.
They want to go home.
No, they were not worried to such an extent [? and ?] you mean they would be exterminated.
Nobody thought about that.
There in Belgium there was a concentration camp called Breendonk.
But that was a concentration camp for people who were punished of doing something.
They put us on transport.
They told us to mark our luggage, that we can find it again, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera.
And they made me to what you call-- each wagon had a transport leader.
So I was a transport leader with a little thing on my wagon-- to help people, to help the elderly, to help the children, to speak in behalf of the people.
Though when we came to the thing-- when we came to the ramp, they just filled up the wagons.
We couldn't believe it.
And they keeped on filling it and filling it and filling it till nobody could move.
They told us, they-- we meeting other trains.
They're short on trains.
And we're going to be trans-shipped into other trains-- to passenger trains.
No.
No, the food goes separate.
Only hour or two-- and they locked the doors.
The Grune Polizei was coming with the-- with the placards, all in green uniforms.
They were lying on top of the trains, all over in every train, on top.
And off we went.
And that was it.
Never ended.
Never ended-- five days, six days.
People were dying.
People did-- urinated, everything, on the floor, everywhere.
People were screaming.
People were pushing.
I was trying to make peace.
It was not possible.
Talk to them, reason with them, console them, like a-- like a social worker-- separate fights amongst men.
I had two helpers.
We just were too squashed in.
And we went.
And on the route, nuns were begging for the children.
That was the interesting part of it, that we couldn't understand.
Give us your children.
Give-- we had windows about that high to breathe.
A baby would pass through.
And nuns were stretching out the arms to us for the children.
They were standing on the station waiting for the trains to get the children.
Probably some must have given it to them, because what I heard, that a lot of children were brought up in convents, didn't even know they were Jews.
No.
Not on my wagon.
And when somebody died in it-- in the wagon, we had to scream, Oberscharfuhrer, [INAUDIBLE] Germany, you know?
And he said, [GERMAN].
And the door was open and the dead person or the very sick person was taken out.
What do you mean, how?
They just dragged them out.
Yeah.
Put them on a-- put them on a-- one of the [INAUDIBLE].
We never saw them again.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And-- and some of them were gassed in ambulances.
We know that after.
The exhaust pipe from the ambulance was going back into the--
No.
Nobody told us where we going.
Nobody knew where we going.
We read-- we read the signs, you know?
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Nuremberg-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Katowice, which was part of Poland, and [INAUDIBLE]-- we went [INAUDIBLE].
The trains were all up and down.
They were not going straight through.
Some of them had to make detours because of military trains and things like that.
They were chunking us, you know?
Yeah.
Sure, I did.
All the time.
There were-- some of them were standing there.
Some of them were laughing.
Some of them were pointing-- [? they're ?] Jews.
They were written 'Jews' outside with chalk.
Outside the wagons were written, with chalk, 'Jews.' The hilarious thing is if you go to Berlin now, there are two of those wagons exposed as a memory of the Nazi atrocities, out in an open place.
Yeah.
Do you really want me to tell you that?
The reaction of the population was worse in Poland than in Germany.
Laughing, mocking-- I couldn't understand them at that time.
Now, I speak Polish because I learned it there.
But it was worse.
[POLISH].
I mean, we knew [POLISH] was, [POLISH]-- spitting.
Some asked for water, didn't give it.
Germany, they gave water.
In Germany, they gave water, and was chased away by the-- by the police.
Water for the children-- they gave water.
But they were pushed away by the police.
And the police said, they're all criminals in there.
They're all rapists and children molesters.
The police said that.
Mm.
Do you mean the ramp in Auschwitz?
We didn't know about camp, nothing.
There was no camp.
There was a-- there was a station, a big, huge railway station.
And that was it.
And they stopped there, and they stopped there for a while.
And then the doors went open.
Yeah.
Dogs, SS in-- in-- in army uniform, SS, and prisoners with stripes.
No.
No.
No signs-- no signs, and no [? reliable ?] times of trains.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
And that was it.
And then we went out, and the usual--
All right.
All right.
